category_title: Probationary high school students with severe behavioral challenges and resistance to instruction
student_description: These are probationary students who exhibit extreme, sometimes dangerous disruptive behaviors (e.g., destroying property, attempting to set fires) when forced into traditional reading-based history instruction. They feel marginalized and hate school, but possess a strong affinity for art, requiring history to be taught entirely through visual research and artifact creation to maintain engagement and prevent severe behavioral outbursts.

criteria.rationale: These students concentrate and retain information better when exploring history through images rather than text.
criteria.check: The long-term plan incorporates visual research methods, such as tracing, finding high-interest images online, or analyzing pictures to uncover historical stories.

criteria.rationale: Re-creating artifacts transforms their severe resistance into full participation and pride in craftsmanship.
criteria.check: The long-term plan includes opportunities for the student to physically create, draw, or build historical artifacts.

criteria.rationale: Removing art to speed up standard-focused instruction causes immediate regression to destructive behaviors.
criteria.check: The long-term plan prioritizes sustained creative/artistic engagement over rushing through traditional curriculum standards or text-based pacing guides.
